steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Need to pull a single file out of a backup archive — /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz has our old nginx config somewhere inside, should be at etc/nginx/nginx.conf relative to the archive root. Just extract that one file to /home/user/restored/, don't need the rest of the tarball unpacked.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user#

Analysis: I'm starting in /home/user directory. I need to extract a specific file (etc/nginx/nginx.conf) from /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz to /home/user/restored/. First, I should verify the archive exists and check if the target directory exists.
Plan: 1. Check if the backup archive exists
2. Check if the restored directory exists, create it if needed
3. List the contents of the archive to verify the nginx.conf file path
4. Extract only that specific file to the target directory

results.content:
Previous response had warnings:
WARNINGS: - Extra text detected before JSON object

New Terminal Output:

root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user# ls -la /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1797 Apr 29 10:42 /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz
root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user# ls -ld /home/user/restored/
drwxrwxrwx 2 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:42 /home/user/restored/
root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user#

steps.message:
Analysis: Good! The archive exists at /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz and the target directory /home/user/restored/ already exists. Now I need to verify the exact path of the nginx.conf file within the archive before extracting it.
Plan: 1. List the contents of the tar.gz archive to confirm the nginx.conf file path
2. Extract only that specific file to /home/user/restored/ using tar with the appropriate options

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user# tar -tzf /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz | grep nginx.conf
etc/nginx/nginx.conf
root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user#

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! The file exists in the archive at exactly the path specified: etc/nginx/nginx.conf. Now I can extract this single file to /home/user/restored/.
Plan: Use tar with the -xzf options to extract, specify the target directory with -C, and provide the specific file path (etc/nginx/nginx.conf) to extract only that file.

results.content:
New Terminal Output:

root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user# tar -xzf /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz -C /home/user/restored/ etc/nginx/nginx.conf
root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user# ls -la /home/user/restored/etc/nginx/
total 12
drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:43 .
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Apr 29 10:43 ..
-rw-r--r-- 1 root root  173 Apr 29 10:42 nginx.conf
root@c2f14477b2e0:/home/user#

steps.message:
Analysis: Excellent! The file has been successfully extracted. The nginx.conf file is now at /home/user/restored/etc/nginx/nginx.conf. The tar command preserved the directory structure (etc/nginx/) within the restored directory. Let me verify the content of the extracted file to confirm it's the correct nginx configuration.
Plan: Display the contents of the extracted nginx.conf file to verify it was extracted correctly, then mark the task as complete.

Analysis: The task has been completed successfully. I have extracted the single file etc/nginx/nginx.conf from the archive /home/user/backups/services-2024.tar.gz to /home/user/restored/. The file now exists at /home/user/restored/etc/nginx/nginx.conf and contains valid nginx configuration content. The extraction preserved the directory structure as expected.
Plan: The task is complete. The nginx.conf file has been successfully extracted from the tar.gz archive to the target directory without unpacking the entire archive.
